h1Title: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is a histological pattern of glomerular injury in which scarring (sclerosis) affects a portion of some but not all glomeruli — the filtering units of the kidney. It is the single most common cause of primary (idiopathic) nephrotic syndrome in adults in the United States, accounting for roughly 40% of adult nephrotic biopsies. FSGS produces heavy proteinuria (typically more than 3.5 g per day), hypoalbuminemia, edema, and progressive kidney function loss. Without treatment, more than 50% of patients with persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria reach end-stage renal disease within 10 years. Immunosuppression with corticosteroids is first-line; resistant cases require calcineurin inhibitors or other agents guided by KDIGO guidelines.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence approximately 7 per million population per year in the US; accounts for ~40% of primary nephrotic syndrome biopsies in adults (USRDS 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Black Americans have 2-4x higher incidence and faster progression than White Americans; secondary FSGS is more common in adults with obesity, HIV, or reduced nephron mass
keyStats.avgAge: Primary FSGS peak onset ages 30-50; secondary forms throughout adult life; genetic FSGS can present in childhood or young adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated to cause end-stage renal disease in 20,000-30,000 patients per year in the United States; global incidence is rising

definition:
Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (ICD-10: N04.1) is a kidney disorder defined histologically by segmental obliteration of the capillary tuft in a subset of glomeruli, accompanied by mesangial expansion, hyalinosis, and podocyte depletion. The word focal means fewer than all glomeruli are affected; segmental means less than the entire glomerular tuft is involved. This pattern can arise from multiple underlying mechanisms and is therefore classified into five subtypes by the Columbia classification: NOS (not otherwise specified), perihilar, cellular, tip, and collapsing — each carrying different clinical associations and prognoses.

The cellular injury is centered on the podocyte, a specialized epithelial cell that forms the slit diaphragm maintaining glomerular permeability. Podocyte loss or dysfunction allows proteins — principally albumin — to cross the filtration barrier, producing the massive proteinuria that defines nephrotic syndrome. FSGS is divided pathophysiologically into primary (idiopathic), secondary (from identifiable causes such as obesity, HIV, heroin use, or reduced nephron mass), and genetic (mutations in podocyte genes including NPHS1, NPHS2, INF2, and TRPC6). Nephrologists diagnose and manage FSGS, with kidney biopsy being essential for classification, since clinical features alone cannot distinguish FSGS subtypes or reliably predict steroid responsiveness.

typesClassification.description: No identifiable cause; likely driven by a circulating permeability factor — possibly soluble urokinase plasminogen activator receptor (suPAR) — that injures podocytes. Presents with abrupt-onset full nephrotic syndrome and highest recurrence rate post-transplant (30-50%).

Arises as a response to sustained hyperfiltration (obesity, single kidney, severe hypertension, sickle cell disease), direct toxin injury (heroin, pamidronate, lithium, anabolic steroids), or HIV infection. Typically presents with less severe proteinuria, less edema, and slower progression than primary FSGS.

Caused by pathogenic variants in podocyte structural genes (NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1, INF2, TRPC6, ACTN4, and others). More common in children and young adults; generally steroid-resistant. Genetic diagnosis avoids unnecessary immunosuppression and guides transplant counseling.

Aggressive subtype with global capillary tuft collapse and podocyte hypertrophy. Strongly associated with HIV (originally termed HIV-associated nephropathy, HIVAN), SARS-CoV-2, and high-risk APOL1 genotype variants in Black patients. Rapid progression to ESRD.
Sclerosis confined to the tubular pole of the glomerulus. Better prognosis than other subtypes; often steroid-responsive. More common in White patients with acute-onset full nephrotic syndrome.

primarySymptoms: Severe proteinuria producing foamy or frothy urine — the most consistent presenting symptom, reflecting albumin and other large proteins crossing the damaged glomerular barrier.
Peripheral edema — pitting swelling of the ankles, legs, and sometimes face, caused by low serum albumin reducing oncotic pressure; typically progresses over weeks to months.
Hypoalbuminemia (serum albumin usually below 3.0 g/dL) found on blood tests, causing soft-tissue edema and contributing to hyperlipidemia.
Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance, driven by low albumin, edema, anemia of chronic kidney disease, and metabolic effects of heavy proteinuria.
Frothy, yellow-tinged, or tea-colored urine — froth from heavy proteinuria; brown discoloration can indicate concurrent hematuria from glomerular inflammation.
Weight gain due to fluid retention, often several kilograms over weeks, masking ongoing muscle wasting from urinary protein loss.
Hypertension, frequently present at diagnosis, driven by sodium retention, volume expansion, and activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.
Hyperlipidemia — elevated total cholesterol and LDL resulting from upregulated hepatic lipoprotein synthesis compensating for low oncotic pressure.
Reduced urine output or oliguria in rapidly progressive or collapsing FSGS variants.
Recurrent infections such as spontaneous bacterial peritonitis or cellulitis, from urinary loss of IgG and complement components.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent foam or froth in toilet water after urination — a simple early sign of significant proteinuria warranting a urine dipstick and albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) test.
Ankle or periorbital swelling appearing gradually without an obvious cause such as recent flight, injury, or cardiac disease.
Unexplained rise in serum creatinine on routine blood work in a patient with hypertension or obesity, suggesting hyperfiltration injury.
Detection of 3+ or 4+ protein on urine dipstick at any medical visit — this requires quantification with ACR and referral for nephrology evaluation.
emergencySigns: Acute kidney injury with creatinine rising more than 0.5 mg/dL per day, particularly in collapsing FSGS — requires urgent nephrology assessment and possible kidney biopsy.
Anasarca (generalized edema including ascites and pleural effusion) with respiratory distress from hypoalbuminemia below 1.5 g/dL — needs inpatient albumin infusion and diuresis.
Signs of renal vein thrombosis: sudden flank pain, hematuria, and worsening creatinine in a patient with nephrotic syndrome — a coagulation emergency requiring anticoagulation.
Sepsis in a nephrotic patient — peritonitis, pneumococcal sepsis, or cellulitis — driven by loss of complement and immunoglobulin in urine; requires prompt antibiotics and hospitalization.

causes.description: Unidentified plasma factor(s) — candidates include soluble urokinase plasminogen activator receptor (suPAR) and others — target podocyte foot processes in primary FSGS, causing effacement and detachment. This explains the high recurrence rate after kidney transplantation.

causes.cause: Glomerular hyperfiltration from reduced nephron mass
causes.description: Single kidney, surgically reduced kidney mass, severe obesity (BMI >40), sickle cell disease, and longstanding hypertension force remaining nephrons to filter excessively, raising intraglomerular pressure and triggering podocyte stress and secondary FSGS.

HIV directly infects renal tubular and podocyte cells. Collapsing FSGS (HIV-associated nephropathy, HIVAN) develops in patients with high HIV viral load, particularly those with APOL1 high-risk genotypes. Effective antiretroviral therapy dramatically reduces incidence.

Pathogenic variants in NPHS2 (podocin), NPHS1 (nephrin), INF2, TRPC6, WT1, and ACTN4 disrupt the slit diaphragm or podocyte cytoskeleton. Genetic testing is now recommended for steroid-resistant FSGS, childhood-onset FSGS, and familial cases.

Heroin use, pamidronate (bisphosphonate therapy), lithium, and anabolic steroids are associated with secondary FSGS. The mechanism includes direct podocyte toxicity and ischemic tubular injury. Heroin-associated nephropathy is a collapsing variant.

Two protective variants against African sleeping sickness — G1 and G2 in the APOL1 gene — dramatically increase FSGS and collapsing nephropathy risk. Individuals with two APOL1 high-risk alleles (G1/G1, G2/G2, or G1/G2) have a 5-17x higher lifetime risk of FSGS and ESRD.

riskFactors.factor: African American / Black ancestry with APOL1 high-risk genotype
riskFactors.description: Homozygous or compound-heterozygous APOL1 high-risk alleles increase FSGS risk 5-17x and accelerate progression. Present in approximately 13% of Black Americans and a significant proportion of West African-ancestry populations.
Severe obesity drives glomerular hyperfiltration and secondary FSGS. Weight loss of 5-10% body weight reduces proteinuria by up to 30-50% in obesity-related FSGS.
HIV viremia, especially with APOL1 high-risk variants, causes collapsing FSGS. Effective ART reduces HIVAN incidence by over 60%.

riskFactors.factor: Single kidney or prior kidney surgery
riskFactors.description: Reduced nephron mass from unilateral nephrectomy, renal hypoplasia, or previous surgery increases residual glomerular filtration pressure, predisposing to FSGS over years to decades.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of FSGS or ESRD at young age
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of patients with FSGS have a 2-3x higher risk, suggesting genetic contributions from podocin, nephrin, and APOL1 variants.

riskFactors.factor: Prior episode of minimal change disease
riskFactors.description: Some adults with initial steroid-sensitive nephrotic syndrome develop FSGS on subsequent biopsies; true incidence of this progression is debated but may be 10-15% over 10 years.

affectedDemographics: Black Americans: incidence 2-4x higher than White Americans due to APOL1 G1/G2 allele frequency; collapsing FSGS disproportionately affects this group.
Adults aged 25-55 are most commonly affected in primary FSGS; genetic FSGS presents in children or young adults.
People living with HIV, particularly those with high viral loads and APOL1 high-risk genotypes, have substantially elevated risk of collapsing FSGS.
Patients with class III-IV obesity (BMI ≥40) account for a growing proportion of secondary FSGS biopsies in developed countries.
diagnosisOverview:
FSGS cannot be diagnosed without kidney biopsy because clinical features — heavy proteinuria, edema, and hypoalbuminemia — are shared by other glomerular diseases including minimal change disease and membranous nephropathy. The diagnostic workup begins with quantifying proteinuria using a spot urine albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) or a 24-hour urine collection, and estimating GFR from serum creatinine using the CKD-EPI formula.

Secondary causes are systematically excluded: HIV testing, hepatitis B and C serologies, ANA and anti-dsDNA for lupus, complement levels, serum electrophoresis for paraprotein, and metabolic parameters for obesity-related hyperfiltration. Genetic testing with a podocytopathy panel (covering NPHS1, NPHS2, INF2, TRPC6, WT1, ACTN4, APOL1, and others) is recommended by KDIGO 2021 guidance for patients with steroid-resistant FSGS, family history, or childhood onset.

Kidney biopsy with light microscopy, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy is the cornerstone. Light microscopy identifies the Columbia classification subtype. Electron microscopy quantifies podocyte foot-process effacement — extensive effacement (>80%) favors primary disease; segmental effacement with intact foot processes in non-sclerotic areas favors secondary FSGS. Immunofluorescence is typically negative or shows nonspecific IgM and C3 in the sclerotic segments, distinguishing FSGS from immune-complex nephritis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies proteinuria; ACR ≥300 mg/g (nephrotic-range) indicates significant glomerular disease requiring kidney biopsy evaluation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A first-morning urine sample collected in a small cup. No special preparation. Results within 24 hours. Values above 2,000 mg/g are typical in FSGS presenting as full nephrotic syndrome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms hypoalbuminemia and hyperlipidemia of nephrotic syndrome; guides need for anticoagulation assessment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard fasting blood draw. Serum albumin below 3.0 g/dL combined with heavy proteinuria confirms nephrotic syndrome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis; distinguishes FSGS subtypes and identifies secondary causes; determines steroid-resistance likelihood
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed with ultrasound or CT guidance under local anesthesia. Two passes of a spring-loaded needle through the skin into the kidney. Observation period of 6-8 hours post-procedure. Risk of significant bleeding in approximately 1 in 200 biopsies.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies high-risk G1/G2 variants that predict faster progression and collapsing FSGS; relevant for Black or West African-ancestry patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood sample sent to a reference genetics laboratory. Results in 2-4 weeks. Genetic counseling is recommended before testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes viral causes of secondary FSGS before committing to immunosuppression, which could worsen viral infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. HIV 4th-generation Ag/Ab test and hepatitis PCR results usually within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies pathogenic variants in NPHS1, NPHS2, INF2, TRPC6, and related genes in patients with steroid-resistant or familial FSGS
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample sent for next-generation sequencing. Results in 3-6 weeks. A positive result changes management, avoiding unnecessary immunosuppression.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment strategy depends entirely on the underlying cause. For primary FSGS with full nephrotic syndrome, KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Disease Guidelines recommend high-dose oral prednisone (1 mg/kg/day up to 80 mg, or 2 mg/kg on alternate days) continued for at least 16 weeks before declaring steroid resistance. Complete remission (proteinuria under 0.3 g/day) or partial remission (50% reduction with sub-nephrotic range) is the target. Only 25-45% of primary FSGS patients achieve complete remission with steroids alone.

For steroid-resistant or steroid-dependent primary FSGS, calcineurin inhibitors — tacrolimus (0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day in two divided doses, targeting trough 5-10 ng/mL) or cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day) — are first-choice second-line agents per KDIGO 2021. Calcineurin inhibitors achieve complete or partial remission in 70-85% of steroid-resistant cases within 6 months. They must be continued for at least 12 months after remission to reduce relapse risk.

Mycophenolate mofetil plus high-dose dexamethasone is an alternative in patients who cannot tolerate calcineurin inhibitors. Rituximab (an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody) is used for steroid-dependent FSGS with frequent relapses. Sparsentan, a dual angiotensin/endothelin receptor antagonist, received FDA accelerated approval in 2023 for reducing proteinuria in primary FSGS.

For all forms: ACE inhibitors or ARBs are cornerstone non-immunosuppressive therapy reducing intraglomerular pressure and proteinuria by 30-50%. Sodium restriction to less than 2 g/day and loop diuretics control edema. Statins treat hyperlipidemia. Anticoagulation is considered when serum albumin falls below 2.0-2.5 g/dL and there is additional thrombotic risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisone (1 mg/kg/day up to 80 mg orally)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line immunosuppression for primary FSGS with full nephrotic syndrome. Continued for a minimum 16 weeks before declaring resistance. Tapered slowly after complete remission to reduce relapse risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete remission in 25-45% of primary FSGS patients; tip lesion subtype responds at 60-80%

medicalTreatments.name: Tacrolimus (trough target 5-10 ng/mL) or cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitors are standard second-line therapy for steroid-resistant primary FSGS per KDIGO 2021. Combine with low-dose prednisone. Nephrotoxicity limits long-term use; monitoring of renal function and drug levels required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission in 70-85% of steroid-resistant primary FSGS at 6 months

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors or ARBs (e.g., ramipril 5-10 mg daily or losartan 50-100 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Cornerstone therapy for all FSGS regardless of immunosuppression. Reduce intraglomerular pressure, proteinuria, and progression rate. Target systolic blood pressure below 125 mmHg in proteinuric patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce proteinuria by 30-50% and slow GFR decline by approximately 25-30% in long-term trials

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil (1.5-2 g/day in two divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for steroid-dependent or calcineurin-intolerant primary FSGS, often combined with low-dose prednisone or dexamethasone. Also considered in secondary FSGS if immunosuppression is warranted.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission in 40-60% when combined with dexamethasone in steroid-resistant cases

medicalTreatments.name: Sparsentan (400-800 mg orally once daily)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA accelerated-approved in 2023 for primary FSGS. Dual endothelin A and angiotensin II receptor antagonist reducing intraglomerular pressure via complementary pathways. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: DUPLEX trial 2023: 42% vs 26% complete remission at 48 weeks vs standard ARB

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (375 mg/m² IV, one to four doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 B-cell depleting agent used for frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent FSGS refractory to other agents. Also studied in post-transplant FSGS recurrence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains remission in 60-70% of frequently relapsing nephrotic syndrome at 12 months in case series and small RCTs

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-sodium, protein-restricted diet (0.8 g/kg/day protein)
alternativeTreatments.description: Reducing dietary protein moderately reduces proteinuria and slows GFR decline in CKD; recommended by KDIGO as an adjunct to pharmacological therapy in progressive FSGS.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with nephrotic-range proteinuria (ACR ≥300 mg/g or spot protein ≥3 g/g) should be referred to a nephrologist urgently, as kidney biopsy is required for diagnosis and treatment decisions cannot be made without histological subtyping. Genetic counseling is warranted in steroid-resistant or familial cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a nephrologist with experience in glomerular disease and steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome, preferably affiliated with a center performing high volumes of kidney biopsies and offering genetic podocytopathy testing. For post-transplant FSGS recurrence, transplant nephrology expertise is essential.
hospitalCriteria: Capability to perform kidney biopsy with real-time ultrasound guidance and on-site pathology with electron microscopy
Access to APOL1 genotyping and a podocytopathy genetic panel
Transplant nephrology service for patients approaching ESRD
Pharmacy able to provide tacrolimus with therapeutic drug monitoring
keyFacilities: Academic medical center nephrology divisions with glomerular disease programs
Kidney biopsy centers with electron microscopy capability
Transplant centers experienced in FSGS recurrence management
preventionStrategies: Treat HIV with effective antiretroviral therapy early — HIVAN incidence drops by over 60% when viral load is suppressed below 50 copies/mL.
Achieve and maintain a healthy body weight (BMI 20-25) to prevent obesity-related hyperfiltration and secondary FSGS.
Control blood pressure to below 130/80 mmHg using ACE inhibitors or ARBs, which protect remaining nephron mass.
Avoid nephrotoxic drugs — heroin, pamidronate at high cumulative doses, lithium, and anabolic steroids — particularly in patients with genetic or familial risk.
In patients with a single functioning kidney, minimize additional glomerular stressors: maintain strict blood pressure control, avoid NSAIDs, and limit dietary protein to 0.8 g/kg/day.
lifestyleModifications: Restrict dietary sodium to less than 2 g per day to control edema and reduce the diuretic dose needed.
Moderate protein intake to 0.8 g/kg/day — excessive protein accelerates glomerular filtration pressure and proteinuria.
Fluid restriction to 1.5-2 L per day during periods of active nephrotic syndrome with severe hypoalbuminemia.
Weigh daily and record results — a gain of more than 1 kg overnight signals fluid accumulation requiring diuretic adjustment.
Take prescribed ACE inhibitor or ARB consistently every day, including when feeling well, as the kidney-protective effect depends on continuous RAAS blockade.
Avoid prolonged inactivity during nephrotic syndrome due to deep vein thrombosis risk from a hypercoagulable state.

recommended: Low-sodium foods (under 2 g sodium daily) to control fluid retention: fresh herbs, spices, vinegar as flavor substitutes for salt
High-quality protein at 0.8 g/kg/day: lean poultry, fish, eggs, legumes
Potassium-rich foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes) if not hyperkalemic — ACE inhibitors and declining GFR can raise potassium
High-fiber foods (oats, legumes, vegetables) to manage hyperlipidemia associated with nephrotic syndrome
avoid: High-sodium processed foods, canned soups, deli meats, and fast food — sodium drives fluid retention
High-potassium foods (oranges, bananas, tomato products) if serum potassium exceeds 5.0 mEq/L on ACE inhibitor
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) — reduce GFR and worsen proteinuria in glomerular disease
Alcohol in excess — raises blood pressure and may contribute to proteinuria and cardiovascular risk

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate aerobic exercise (30 minutes of walking, cycling, or swimming, most days) is encouraged once edema is controlled. Heavy resistance exercise that raises blood pressure acutely should be avoided during active nephrotic syndrome. Physical activity independently reduces proteinuria and cardiovascular risk in CKD patients.
dailyManagement: Take all prescribed medications — ACE inhibitor, diuretic, statin, immunosuppressant — at consistent times daily.
Monitor blood pressure daily with a home cuff; target below 130/80 mmHg; report readings consistently above 140/90 mmHg.
Weigh each morning after urinating; contact the care team if weight increases more than 2 kg in 48 hours.
Track urine output volume and foaminess — increased foam or reduced output signals worsening proteinuria or kidney function.
Attend scheduled nephrology appointments for serum creatinine, potassium, ACR, drug-level monitoring (if on calcineurin inhibitors), and blood pressure review.
Maintain updated vaccination records — pneumococcal (PPSV23 and PCV15/20), annual influenza, and hepatitis B vaccines are especially important in nephrotic patients receiving immunosuppression.
prognosis: Prognosis is strongly linked to the FSGS subtype, initial response to treatment, and underlying cause. Complete remission following corticosteroids confers excellent kidney survival — approximately 90% at 10 years. Partial remission carries a 10-year kidney survival of 65-70%. Patients with persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria who fail all immunosuppressive therapy progress to end-stage renal disease in a median 5-10 years in most cohort studies. Collapsing FSGS carries the worst prognosis: median time to ESRD is 2-3 years without treatment. Black patients with two APOL1 high-risk alleles progress to ESRD at twice the rate of those with zero or one risk allele, regardless of treatment. Kidney transplantation is the treatment of choice for ESRD, but primary FSGS recurs in 30-50% of transplants, often within the first days to weeks after transplantation, potentially threatening graft survival.
recoveryTimeline: Complete remission after steroid therapy typically occurs within 8-16 weeks. Partial responders require 6-12 months of calcineurin inhibitor therapy to achieve full remission. Secondary FSGS from obesity may improve within 6-12 months of weight loss. Long-term follow-up with annual GFR and ACR monitoring is lifelong for all patients.
complications: End-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplantation — occurs in more than 50% of patients with persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria over 10 years.
Venous thromboembolism including renal vein thrombosis — driven by urinary loss of anticoagulant proteins (antithrombin III, protein S); risk especially high when albumin is below 2.0 g/dL.
Hyperlipidemia-related atherosclerosis — nephrotic-range proteinuria raises LDL cholesterol and accelerates cardiovascular disease; statin therapy is indicated.
Post-transplant FSGS recurrence — occurs in 30-50% of patients with primary FSGS, often within hours to days, and may require plasmapheresis or rituximab.
Serious infections — loss of immunoglobulins and complement in urine and immunosuppressive therapy increase susceptibility to encapsulated bacteria and opportunistic infections.
Chronic kidney disease progression with anemia, mineral bone disease, and hypertension requiring multidisciplinary management.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation — FSGS Resource Center
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on FSGS diagnosis, treatment options, and living with kidney disease.

supportResources.name: KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Disease Guidelines
supportResources.description: International clinical practice guidelines for FSGS and other glomerular diseases, freely available for patients and clinicians.

supportResources.name: Nephrotic Syndrome Study Network (NEPTUNE)
supportResources.description: NIH-funded patient registry and research network for FSGS and related nephrotic conditions; includes patient education resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Minimal Change Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Minimal change disease (MCD) and FSGS both produce full nephrotic syndrome, but MCD shows only diffuse podocyte foot-process effacement on electron microscopy with no sclerotic lesions on light microscopy. MCD is almost universally steroid-responsive (90% remission rate) whereas only 25-45% of primary FSGS responds. Distinguishing the two requires kidney biopsy with electron microscopy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Membranous nephropathy causes nephrotic syndrome through subepithelial immune-complex deposits and a thickened glomerular basement membrane — distinct from the sclerotic lesions of FSGS. Membranous nephropathy is strongly associated with PLA2R antibody in 70-80% of primary cases; FSGS has no consistent serological marker. Electron microscopy differentiates definitively.

confusedWithConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IgA nephropathy typically presents with hematuria and moderate proteinuria after upper respiratory infections; immunofluorescence shows mesangial IgA deposits absent in FSGS. While IgA nephropathy can progress to FSGS-like scarring, it has a distinct pathogenesis and requires different immunosuppressive management.

relatedConditions.name: Nephrotic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: FSGS is the leading cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in US adults; the clinical presentation of heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, and edema defines nephrotic syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Minimal Change Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The key differential diagnosis for nephrotic syndrome in both children and adults; distinguished from FSGS only by kidney biopsy.

relatedConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Another major cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in adults; distinguished by subepithelial deposits and PLA2R antibody.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Lupus nephritis can produce a secondary FSGS pattern on biopsy; systemic lupus erythematosus evaluation is part of the FSGS workup.

relatedConditions.name: End-Stage Renal Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The most feared outcome of treatment-resistant FSGS; FSGS is one of the leading causes of ESRD in Black Americans.

faqs.question: What is focal segmental glomerulosclerosis?
faqs.answer: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is a kidney disease where scarring affects parts of some — but not all — of the glomeruli (filtering units). This scarring allows massive protein leakage into the urine, causing nephrotic syndrome with edema, low albumin, and high cholesterol. It is the most common cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in US adults.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of FSGS?
faqs.answer: The hallmark symptoms are foamy urine from heavy protein leakage, swelling of the legs and face, weight gain from fluid retention, and fatigue. High blood pressure and high cholesterol are also common. Blood tests show low albumin and elevated creatinine if kidney function is declining.

faqs.question: How is FSGS diagnosed?
faqs.answer: FSGS requires kidney biopsy because clinical features are shared with other glomerular diseases. A biopsy with light microscopy, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy identifies the FSGS pattern and subtype. Secondary causes (HIV, lupus, obesity) and genetic mutations are excluded before diagnosis.

faqs.question: What causes FSGS?
faqs.answer: Primary FSGS is thought to be caused by a circulating permeability factor that injures podocytes. Secondary causes include obesity, HIV, reduced kidney mass, and drugs. Genetic mutations in podocyte proteins (NPHS1, NPHS2) cause hereditary FSGS. APOL1 gene variants increase risk in people of African ancestry.

faqs.question: Can FSGS be cured?
faqs.answer: Complete remission (very low proteinuria) is achieved in 25-45% of primary FSGS patients with steroids and up to 85% with calcineurin inhibitors. Remission does not always mean cure — relapse occurs in 30-50% of cases. Patients who cannot achieve remission are at high risk of reaching end-stage kidney disease over 5-10 years.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for FSGS?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment for primary FSGS with nephrotic syndrome is high-dose prednisone for 16 weeks. Steroid-resistant cases receive calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus or cyclosporine). ACE inhibitors or ARBs are given to all patients to reduce proteinuria. Sparsentan was FDA-approved in 2023 as an additional option.

faqs.question: What is steroid-resistant FSGS?
faqs.answer: Steroid resistance means proteinuria does not fall to remission levels after at least 16 weeks of adequate prednisone therapy. It occurs in 55-75% of primary FSGS patients. Steroid-resistant patients need second-line agents such as tacrolimus or cyclosporine, and genetic testing is recommended.

faqs.question: Does FSGS cause kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Yes, without treatment or in refractory disease. More than 50% of patients with persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria progress to end-stage renal disease within 10 years. Collapsing FSGS is the most aggressive variant, often reaching ESRD within 2-3 years.

faqs.question: Can FSGS come back after a kidney transplant?
faqs.answer: Yes. Primary FSGS recurs in 30-50% of kidney transplants, sometimes within hours to days of transplantation due to the circulating permeability factor attacking the new kidney. Recurrence can be treated with plasmapheresis and rituximab but may cause graft failure.

faqs.question: Is FSGS hereditary?
faqs.answer: Genetic forms of FSGS, caused by mutations in NPHS1, NPHS2, INF2, TRPC6, and other podocyte genes, account for 10-30% of steroid-resistant and pediatric cases. APOL1 gene variants in people of African descent significantly increase lifetime risk without being directly causal.

faqs.question: What does foamy urine mean in FSGS?
faqs.answer: Foamy or frothy urine indicates heavy protein in the urine (proteinuria). In FSGS, damaged glomeruli allow large amounts of albumin to leak into the urine, creating persistent foam after urination. This is an early warning sign that should be checked with a urine test.

faqs.question: Is FSGS the same as nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: No, but FSGS is the most common cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in US adults. Nephrotic syndrome is the clinical syndrome of heavy proteinuria, low albumin, edema, and high cholesterol. FSGS is one of several kidney diseases that produce this syndrome; the distinction requires biopsy.

faqs.question: What diet is recommended for FSGS?
faqs.answer: A low-sodium diet under 2 g per day reduces fluid retention. Moderate protein intake of 0.8 g per kilogram of body weight per day slows progression. High-fiber foods and statins manage the high cholesterol that comes with nephrotic syndrome. Potassium intake may need adjustment if ACE inhibitors are prescribed.

faqs.question: Are Black Americans at higher risk of FSGS?
faqs.answer: Yes. Black Americans have 2-4x higher incidence of FSGS than White Americans, largely due to a higher frequency of APOL1 G1 and G2 gene variants. These variants raise lifetime FSGS risk 5-17x and are associated with faster progression to kidney failure.

faqs.question: What is collapsing FSGS?
faqs.answer: Collapsing FSGS is an aggressive variant where the glomerular tuft collapses globally. It is strongly associated with HIV infection (HIVAN), SARS-CoV-2, and the APOL1 high-risk genotype. It progresses to ESRD in a median 2-3 years without treatment and does not respond well to standard steroids.

metaTitle: FSGS Kidney Disease: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: FSGS causes nephrotic syndrome with foamy urine, edema, and kidney failure risk. Causes, biopsy diagnosis, steroid and calcineurin inhibitor treatment reviewed.
